townstead.pose.picker.title: Choose a pose
townstead.configuration.title: %s Configuration
townstead.configuration.needs.tooltip: Controls villager hunger and thirst systems.
townstead.configuration.needs.hunger.too: Controls how villagers source and manage hunger.
townstead.configuration.needs.hunger.ena: Enable villager hunger simulation. When disabled, villagers skip hunger decay, eating behaviors, and hunger-driven mood drift.
Allow villagers to eat food they are already carrying.
Allow villagers to collect edible dropped items from the ground.
Allow villagers to take food from nearby inventories and item handlers.
Allow villagers to harvest mature crops as an emergency food fallback. Disabled by default to reduce broad crop scans.
Let villagers teleport when they eat chorus fruit, just like players do.
Let villagers put empty containers left from eating/drinking (bowls, bottles, buckets) into nearby storage, preferring kitchen storage. Skipped while actively working.
townstead.configuration.needs.thirst.too: Controls how villagers source and manage thirst when Thirst Was Taken is installed.
townstead.configuration.needs.thirst.ena: Allow villagers to drink thirst-restoring items they are already carrying.
Allow villagers to collect thirst-restoring dropped items from the ground.
Allow villagers to take thirst-restoring items from nearby inventories and item handlers.
Allow villagers to harvest mature crops as an emergency thirst fallback. Disabled by default to reduce broad crop scans.
Enable villager thirst simulation when Thirst Was Taken is installed.
townstead.configuration.needs.thirst.thi: Allow dehydration to kill villagers when hardcore status cannot be detected.
townstead.configuration.needs.thirst.ena: Allow cooks to opportunistically purify impure water bottles in available kitchen skillets.
townstead.configuration.needs.thirst.pre: Prefer depositing empty bottles into kitchen storage after drinking.
townstead.configuration.farming.tooltip: Controls farmer villager AI behavior and farm parameters.
townstead.configuration.farming.enableFa: Adds anti-trample and idle unstuck nudges for harvest chore villagers.
townstead.configuration.farming.farmerFa: Max horizontal working radius around the farm anchor.
townstead.configuration.farming.farmerCe: How long before a villager can rework the same soil cell.
townstead.configuration.farming.farmerPa: Pathing retries per target before temporary blacklist.
townstead.configuration.farming.farmerId: How long villagers wait before reacquiring work after no valid target.
townstead.configuration.farming.enableFa: Allow farmers to place water sources in planned tiles when hydration is too low.
townstead.configuration.farming.farmerWa: How far farmers may travel horizontally to refill water buckets.
Vertical water-source search range used for bucket refills.
townstead.configuration.farming.farmerGr: Radius around planned farm cells where farmers may clear removable weeds.
Ticks between farmer grooming target scans.
townstead.configuration.farming.enableFa: Allow farmers to periodically ask for missing supplies in local chat.
townstead.configuration.farming.farmerRe: Minimum ticks between repeated farmer shortage requests.
townstead.configuration.fishing.enableFi: Allow fishermen to periodically announce missing rods or water in local chat.
townstead.configuration.fishing.fisherma: Minimum ticks between fisherman shortage request messages.
How many blocks away from the barrel to look for water when fishing.
Number of items the fisherman carries before returning to deposit.
townstead.configuration.cooking.enableCo: Allow cooks to periodically announce missing kitchen supplies in local chat.
townstead.configuration.cooking.cookRequ: Minimum ticks between cook shortage request messages.
Controls barista villager AI behavior (requires Rustic Delight).
Allow baristas to periodically announce missing cafe supplies in local chat.
Minimum ticks between barista shortage request messages.
townstead.configuration.butchery.tooltip: Controls butcher villager AI and trade behavior (requires Butchery).
townstead.configuration.butchery.enableV: Allow butchers to slaughter whitelisted livestock in Butchery slaughter areas.
townstead.configuration.butchery.allowHu: Permit villager-driven slaughter of humanoid carcasses (villagers, pillagers, witches). Off by default; the integration does not lean into this even when enabled.
townstead.configuration.butchery.village: Minimum ticks between kills for a single butcher villager.
townstead.configuration.butchery.include: Add a second Master-tier trade pool with exotic cuts (brain, tongue, kidney, sweetbread).
townstead.configuration.butchery.hammerT: When enabled, the butcher auto-hammers rare display-worthy heads (evoker, vindicator, pillager, warden, dragon, player, wither skull, ice skull) into their breakdown drops. Off by default so those heads stay whole for trophies and armor.
townstead.configuration.caregiving.toolt: Controls how villagers feed babies and children.
townstead.configuration.caregiving.enabl: Allow adults to feed hungry babies, toddlers, and children.
Allow nearby non-parent villagers to feed young villagers when parents are absent.
townstead.configuration.storage.tooltip: Controls villager access to storage containers and protected blocks.
townstead.configuration.storage.enableWo: Allows chore restocking and harvest output storage from nearby containers. Disable to preserve baseline MCA chore behavior.
townstead.configuration.storage.enableHa: Allow harvesting villagers to store gathered output in nearby containers.
townstead.configuration.storage.respectP: If enabled, villagers will not take food from protected storage blocks or tags.
townstead.configuration.storage.protecte: List of block IDs villagers must not take food from.
List of block tags treated as protected food storage.
townstead.configuration.chefsdelight_com: Controls integration with Chef's Delight mod.
When enabled, Townstead handles cook AI and profession assignment. Disable to let Chef's Delight handle cooking instead.
townstead.configuration.debug.debugVilla: Enable sleep/rest debug logs and villager debug state updates.
Show debug chat messages for villager AI (farmer, cook, etc.) to nearby players.
townstead.configuration.calendar.profile: Active calendar profile id, or "auto" to detect seasonal mods. Profiles are data-pack-defined; see data/<ns>/calendar_profile/*.json.
townstead.configuration.calendar.randomi: APPLIES ONLY TO NEW WORLDS. Editing this on an existing save has no effect. When enabled, fresh worlds roll a random starting year and day-of-year. Seasonal mods read dayTime, so they pick up the rolled season automatically.
townstead.configuration.calendar.startYe: APPLIES ONLY TO NEW WORLDS. Lower bound (inclusive) for the rolled starting display year.
APPLIES ONLY TO NEW WORLDS. Upper bound (inclusive) for the rolled starting display year. If <= min, only min is used.
townstead.configuration.calendar.realClo: When on, the calendar also advances by real-world time elapsed while the game was off (villagers age while you're away). When off, it tracks Minecraft days only.
townstead.configuration.calendar.agingSc: Game-days per narrative ("dog-years") year — the single, species-neutral aging rate. Every villager ages 1 narrative year per this many game-days; longer-lived races author more narrative years and so take proportionally longer. Calendar-independent. Default 8.0 (a 90-year human life ~720 days). Higher = slower aging.
townstead.configuration.calendar.disable: Villagers stop aging and hold their current life stage. Animals are unaffected.
townstead.configuration.mood_audio.muteM: If enabled, MCA villager laugh/celebrate/cry sounds are muted on this client.
townstead.configuration.accessibility.re: Disables text animation effects (wave, shake, bounce, scale) in dialogue. Emotion colors still apply.
townstead.configuration.accessibility.di: Disables screen-space and world-space particles during dialogue.
Prevents the camera from rotating to face the villager during dialogue.
townstead.configuration.accessibility.sp: Overlays distinct hatching patterns on each spirit's bar so they can be told apart without relying on color.
Narrator speaks the hovered spirit's points, tier, and share when it changes.
Grows row heights and bar thickness on the Spirit page for easier clicking on touch or low-precision setups.
Uses pure black/white text, brighter borders, and stronger hover highlights on the Spirit page.
Multiplier for Spirit page text size. 1.0 = default, 2.0 = double-size.
townstead.configuration.catalog.useTowns: Use the Townstead extended catalog with kitchen building tiers. Disable to use MCA's original catalog.

buildingType.compat/butchery/butcher_sho: A smoker, a butchers' table, and a place to store cuts. Enough for a village butcher to work.
A working butchery with a basin, hanging hook, and prep counter for bleeding and quartering.
A master butcher's shop with grinder, skin rack, pestle, and freezer for the full processing chain.
buildingType.compat/butchery/slaughterho: A dedicated kill floor. Hooks, basins, and drains for handling multiple carcasses at once.
A shared bank of smokers for when one butcher cannot keep up with the village's appetite.
buildingType.compat/butchery/tannery.des: Racks, basins, and a cauldron for turning hides into leather.
buildingType.compat.butchery.butcher_sho: A smoker, a butchers' table, and a place to store cuts. Enough for a village butcher to work.
A working butchery with a basin, hanging hook, and prep counter for bleeding and quartering.
A master butcher's shop with grinder, skin rack, pestle, and freezer for the full processing chain.
buildingType.compat.butchery.slaughterho: A dedicated kill floor. Hooks, basins, and drains for handling multiple carcasses at once.
A shared bank of smokers for when one butcher cannot keep up with the village's appetite.
buildingType.compat.butchery.tannery.des: Racks, basins, and a cauldron for turning hides into leather.
buildingType.compat.butchery.slaughter_p: A fenced enclosure with a blood grate. Butchers only slaughter livestock penned here, leaving other herds alone.
buildingType.compat/butchery/slaughter_p: A fenced enclosure with a blood grate. Butchers only slaughter livestock penned here, leaving other herds alone.
buildingType.pen.description: A fenced enclosure with feed for grazing herds. Kept out of the butcher's reach.
buildingType.wool_shed.description: A loom, a stack of wool, and storage. Where the shepherd brings fresh wool.
buildingType.compat/farmersdelight/kitch: A simple hearth kitchen with heat, one cooking station, and basic storage.
Adds a prep station for better ingredient handling.
A copper-equipped cookhouse built for steady daily production.
A professional stove kitchen with premium prep and upgraded storage.
An elite grand kitchen with multiple stoves, advanced prep, and nether-grade storage.
buildingType.compat.farmersdelight.kitch: A simple hearth kitchen with heat, one cooking station, and basic storage.
Adds a prep station for better ingredient handling.
A copper-equipped cookhouse built for steady daily production.
A professional stove kitchen with premium prep and upgraded storage.
An elite grand kitchen with multiple stoves, advanced prep, and nether-grade storage.
buildingType.dock_l1.description: A plank deck at the water's edge. A fisherman with a nearby barrel prefers to cast from it.
buildingType.dock_l2.description: A proper pier with lights, railings, and posts sunk into the water. Fishing here is noticeably more productive.
buildingType.dock_l3.description: A substantial wharf built over deep water, with abundant lanterns, railings, and pillars. Fishermen get better loot, faster bites, and occasional double catches.
buildingType.compat/rusticdelight/cafe_l: A small café that prepares basic drinks and provides a meeting place for your village.
A more established coffee shop that can serve syrupy drinks and keep up with growing demand.
A proper coffee house with an additional barista and a wider menu of complex drinks.
A well-appointed café hall with stove-brewed specialties and premium decoration.
The finest coffee house a village can offer.
buildingType.compat.rusticdelight.cafe_l: A small café that prepares basic drinks and provides a meeting place for your village.
A more established coffee shop that can serve syrupy drinks and keep up with growing demand.
A proper coffee house with an additional barista and a wider menu of complex drinks.
A well-appointed café hall with stove-brewed specialties and premium decoration.
The finest coffee house a village can offer.
dialogue.chat.hunger.starving/1: I... I can barely stand. Please, do you have anything to eat?
dialogue.chat.hunger.starving/2: Everything's going dark around the edges... I need food, %1$s.
dialogue.chat.hunger.starving/3: I don't know how much longer I can go on like this without a meal...
dialogue.chat.hunger.starving/4: *clutches stomach* ...I'm sorry, I can't focus on anything right now.
dialogue.chat.hunger.starving/5: If I don't eat something soon, I'm going to collapse right here.
dialogue.chat.hunger.starving/7: I can't remember the last time I ate. My hands won't stop shaking.
dialogue.chat.hunger.starving/8: I'd give anything for even a piece of bread right now, %1$s.
dialogue.chat.hunger.famished/1: I haven't eaten in ages... do you have anything?
dialogue.chat.hunger.famished/3: I can't stop thinking about food right now...
dialogue.chat.hunger.famished/4: My stomach has been growling all day. I really need to find something to eat.
dialogue.chat.hunger.famished/5: I'd trade my best boots for a warm meal right about now.
dialogue.chat.hunger.famished/6: Have you seen any food around? I'm getting desperate, %1$s.
dialogue.chat.hunger.famished/7: Sorry if I seem distracted. It's hard to think about anything but food.
dialogue.chat.hunger.famished/8: I keep eyeing the crops in the fields... they can't grow fast enough.
dialogue.chat.hunger.hungry/1: I could really go for a bite to eat, honestly.
dialogue.chat.hunger.hungry/2: Is it lunchtime yet? My stomach seems to think so.
dialogue.chat.hunger.hungry/3: I've been meaning to grab something to eat. Just haven't gotten around to it.
dialogue.chat.hunger.hungry/4: Something smells good around here... or maybe I'm just hungry.
dialogue.chat.hunger.hungry/5: A nice stew would really hit the spot right about now.
dialogue.chat.hunger.hungry/6: I wonder what's for supper tonight? I'm getting a bit peckish.
dialogue.chat.thirst.dehydrated/1: My throat feels like sand... I need water now.
dialogue.chat.thirst.dehydrated/2: Please... water... I can barely speak.
dialogue.chat.thirst.dehydrated/3: I'm so dehydrated I can hardly think straight.
dialogue.chat.thirst.dehydrated/4: Everything tastes like dust right now.
dialogue.chat.thirst.parched/1: I'm parched. Have you seen any clean water?
dialogue.chat.thirst.parched/2: I would trade a meal for a drink right now.
dialogue.chat.thirst.parched/3: My lips are dry and my head hurts. I need a drink.
dialogue.chat.thirst.parched/4: A cool drink would make all the difference right now.
dialogue.chat.thirst.thirsty/1: I could really use something to drink.
dialogue.chat.thirst.thirsty/2: Do we have any water nearby?
dialogue.chat.thirst.thirsty/3: I've been working all day and I'm getting thirsty.
dialogue.chat.thirst.thirsty/4: A sip of water sounds perfect about now.
dialogue.chat.farmer_progress.tier_up/1: I feel like I finally understand this land a little better.
dialogue.chat.farmer_progress.tier_up/2: These fields are starting to make sense to me now.
dialogue.chat.farmer_progress.tier_up/3: I've learned a better way to shape these plots.
dialogue.chat.farmer_progress.tier_up/4: My farming craft just took a step forward.
dialogue.chat.farmer_progress.tier_up/5: I can handle a more advanced farm plan now.
dialogue.chat.farmer_progress.tier_up/6: I've unlocked new farming ideas for our town.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_cafe/1: I need a proper cafe to work in. Could you set one up?
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_cafe/2: I can't brew anything without a cafe to call my own.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_cafe/3: A cooking pot and some storage would make a fine little cafe.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_cafe/4: If you build a cafe, I can start making drinks for everyone.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_ingredi: I'm out of ingredients. Could you stock the cafe?
The shelves are bare... I need coffee beans or something to work with.
No beans, no brew. Can someone bring supplies?
I'd love to make something, but there's nothing to work with here.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_storage: I have nowhere to put the finished drinks.
The cafe needs more storage space.
Could use a chest or barrel to store my brews.
I keep running out of room for the things I make.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.unreachabl: I can't seem to reach my station right now.
Something's blocking the way to my brewing pot.
The path to my cafe station is blocked.
I'll need a clear path to the pot before I can brew.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_recipe/: I don't know any recipes I can make right now.
None of my recipes match what's available.
I need a better-equipped cafe for the drinks I know.
Maybe a cafe upgrade would unlock new recipes for me.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_recipe_: I don't know any recipes for %2$s yet.
dialogue.chat.barista_request.no_ingredi: I want to make %2$s but I'm missing the ingredients.
dialogue.chat.cook_request.no_recipe_ite: I don't know any recipes for %2$s yet.
dialogue.chat.cook_request.no_ingredient: I want to make %2$s but I can't find the ingredients.
townstead.configuration.needs.fatigue.to: Controls villager fatigue and sleep needs.
townstead.configuration.needs.fatigue.en: Show local chat alerts when villagers collapse from exhaustion or recover.
Enable villager fatigue simulation. Villagers accumulate fatigue during activity and recover through rest.
townstead.configuration.needs.fatigue.fa: Fatigue level that triggers collapse when no bed is available.
Fatigue must drop below this level before a villager can resume work after exhaustion.
Fatigue accumulation multiplier when working during aligned cycle hours (lower = less fatigue).
Fatigue accumulation multiplier when working during misaligned cycle hours (higher = more fatigue).
dialogue.chat.fatigue.exhausted/1: I can't... I can't keep my eyes open. I need to lie down.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.exhausted/2: Everything's blurry. I think I'm going to collapse...
dialogue.chat.fatigue.exhausted/3: Please, %1$s, I need a bed. I can barely stand.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.exhausted/4: I haven't slept in... I don't even remember. My legs are giving out.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.exhausted/5: If I don't rest soon, I'm going to fall down right here.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.exhausted/6: I can't work like this. I need sleep more than anything.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.drowsy/1: I keep losing my train of thought... I'm so tired.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.drowsy/2: *yawns deeply* ...sorry. I really need some rest.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.drowsy/3: I could really use a bed right about now.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.drowsy/4: My eyes keep closing on their own. This shift is too long.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.drowsy/5: I'm struggling to focus. When is rest time?
dialogue.chat.fatigue.drowsy/6: A few hours of sleep would make all the difference, %1$s.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.tired/1: I'm getting a bit sleepy. A rest would be nice.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.tired/2: *yawns* ...excuse me. Starting to feel the day catching up.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.tired/3: I wouldn't mind an early night tonight.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.tired/4: Getting a little worn out. Nothing a good nap won't fix.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.tired/5: Is it almost time for rest? I could use the sleep.
dialogue.chat.fatigue.tired/6: The day's been long. I'm looking forward to bed.
dialogue.chat.energy.collapsed/1: I can't go on... I need to rest...
dialogue.chat.energy.collapsed/3: My legs... gave out... need sleep...
dialogue.chat.energy.collapsed/4: I'm done... I can't move another step...
dialogue.chat.energy.recovered/1: *gets up slowly* ...I think I can manage now.
dialogue.chat.energy.recovered/2: Ugh... that was awful. I'm back on my feet, at least.
dialogue.chat.energy.recovered/3: I feel a little better. Let me try to get going again.
townstead.dialogue.main.joke: I've got a joke for you!
townstead.dialogue.main.story: Let me tell you a story...
townstead.dialogue.main.adopt: Would you like to come with me?
townstead.dialogue.main.procreate: Shall we try for a baby?
townstead.dialogue.main.divorceInitiate: We need to talk about us...
townstead.dialogue.joke.animal: So, this animal walks into a bar...
townstead.dialogue.joke.monster: You won't believe what a creeper told me...
townstead.dialogue.story.generic: Let me tell you about something...
townstead.dialogue.hire.short: Just for a few days. (5 emeralds)
townstead.dialogue.first.settling: I'm looking for a place to settle.
townstead.dialogue.first.spent_night: I stayed at the inn last night.
message.townstead.breeding_different_spe: Different species can't have children together.
townstead.field_post.filter.in_stock.too: Show only crops with seeds in village containers or villager inventories
townstead.field_post.toast.mismatch.body: %1$s can't be planted on %2$s. The farmer will skip this cell.
townstead.guide.name: Townstead Guide
townstead.guide.subtitle: Village life
townstead.guide.landing: Welcome to Townstead, the unofficial all-encompassing expansion of Minecraft Comes Alive. This guide covers the various bits of functionality and features that Townstead brings. This is everything from the needs system, the mod integrations, and the expanded UI. If you're not sure where to start, take a look at $(l:townstead:farming/getting_started)Farming$(/l) to explore how to farm with Townstead.
townstead.guide.category.farming.desc: Set up plots with a Field Post so your farmers know what to plant where.
townstead.guide.entry.farming.getting_st: In Townstead, farmers follow the plans you set out for them. You decide exactly which patches of ground are the farm, and they handle the rest: tilling, planting, watering, and harvesting.$(br2)The tool you use to tell them is the $(l:townstead:farming/field_post)Field Post$(/l).
Craft one Field Post and place it where you want to farm. You can have multiple in different areas to expand coverage.
Right-click the Field Post to open the $(l:townstead:farming/plot_planner)Plot Planner$(/l). From there you paint the cells you want farmed, deciding on which crops and soil go where.

Each farmer needs the farmer profession and a composter nearby for their home base. The Field Post covers an area around itself; any farmer whose composter sits inside that area will use the plan.$(br2)They also need tools: hoe, bucket, and seeds. Once they have what they need, they'll work the field on their own and keep planting.
townstead.guide.entry.farming.field_post: The $(l)Field Post$() is the centerpiece of any Townstead farm. It stores a plan for the cells around it, which your farmers read every tick.

Place the Field Post roughly in the center of where you want the farm to be. Its reach extends out 16 blocks.$(br2)Only one Field Post covers any given farmer; if two overlap, the nearest one with the highest priority wins.
Breaking the Field Post cancels the plan. Farmers under its coverage will go idle until you place another one.
townstead.guide.entry.farming.plot_plann: Right-click the Field Post to open the Plot Planner. You'll see a top-down grid of the area around the post, showing the current terrain and any crops already growing.$(br2)On the left is the toolbar. On the right is the grid. Click a tool, then click-and-drag on the grid to paint cells.
There are two layers to the plan:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Crops$() - what crop should go here$(li)$(l)Soil$() - what kind of ground you want here (farmland, rich soil, etc.)

When you're done, click the checkmark to save. If a farmer is working, they'll pick up the updated plan shortly. Click the X to discard changes.$(br2)You can come back and edit any time. Painting over a cell overwrites the old assignment.
townstead.guide.entry.farming.soil_and_c: Available soil types:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Farmland$() - standard tilled dirt. The farmer tills with a hoe if needed.$(li)$(l)Rich Soil$() - upgraded farmland (requires Farmer's Delight). The farmer tills as normal, then upgrades the block.$(li)$(l)None$() - soil is left alone. Farmer won't till here, but can still plant or harvest if already tilled.

Available crop modes:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Auto$() - farmer picks a sensible crop from their inventory based on personality.$(li)$(l)Specific crop$() - only that crop gets planted here (wheat, carrot, whatever you picked).

$(l)Protected$() is a special option for both layers. A protected cell is completely off-limits to the farmer. They won't till, plant, water, harvest, or groom it.$(br2)Use this for decorative crops, your personal wheat patch, or anything you want to manage yourself. Protected cells still show up in the grid but are invisible to the AI.
townstead.guide.entry.farming.how_farmer: For each painted cell, the farmer checks what that cell currently needs and picks the most pressing task. The order is:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Harvest$() if there's a ripe crop$(li)$(l)Till$() if the soil isn't farmland yet$(li)$(l)Plant$() if the soil is ready but empty$(li)$(l)Place water$() if the cell is painted Water and dry$(li)$(l)Groom$() weeds next to cells

The farmer picks the nearest cell with a pending task and walks to it. Once that's done, they pick the next nearest task. This keeps them moving in a tight loop rather than running back and forth across the whole field.

The farmer's behavior is driven entirely by the plan. They don't notice farmland you placed outside the painted area, and they won't invent new rows on their own. If you want them to work a patch, paint it.

$(l)Player work is respected.$() If you till a cell yourself, the farmer sees it's already tilled and moves on to planting. Same if you plant a crop yourself, they'll water and harvest it when ready. You can prep as much of the farm as you like; the plan tells them what's in scope.
townstead.guide.entry.farming.water_and_: Farmers place water where the plan tells them to. If a cell is marked for water and the farmer has a bucket, they'll place it, even if there's already a water source nearby.

Grass, ferns, and similar weeds next to your painted cells will get pulled. This is mostly cosmetic, but with $(l)Farmer's Delight$() installed and a knife in the farmer's inventory, grooming tall grass drops rope too.$(br2)Grooming is bounded to within 1 block of painted cells, so the farmer won't wander off to weed your lawn.
townstead.guide.entry.farming.troublesho: The farmer can't find a Field Post covering their composter. Place one nearby and paint some cells.
The farmer has no seeds in their inventory. Drop some in a chest near their composter, or hand them some directly.
They need a hoe to till. A wooden one is enough. You can leave one in a nearby chest.
Pathfinding can't get them to a cell. Check for obstructions, fences, or soil cells placed too high up. Try removing any barriers between the composter and the farm.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.overview.pag: Every villager tracks three needs: hunger, thirst, and energy. When all three are topped up, villagers work well and stay in a good mood. When one of them runs out, problems follow.

- $(l)Hunger$() - 0 to 100. Drops from work and time. Refilled by eating.$(li)$(l)Thirst$() - 0 to 20. Drops slowly on its own, faster from work. Refilled by drinking.$(li)$(l)Energy$() - 0 to 20 fatigue. Drops from work, rises from sleep.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.hunger.page1: Hunger runs from 0 to 100. Villagers start at 80 and lose points over time. At low values they slow down, get grumpy, and eventually starve.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.hunger.page2: The top two bands are safe:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Well-Fed$() (80-100) - small mood boost$(li)$(l)Adequate$() (50-79) - no effect
townstead.guide.entry.needs.hunger.page3: Below 50, things get worse:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Hungry$() (25-49) - mood drops, villager seeks food$(li)$(l)Famished$() (1-24) - mood drops harder, movement -25%%$(li)$(l)Starving$() (0) - large mood penalty, no work
townstead.guide.entry.needs.hunger.page4: A hungry villager looks for food in this order:$(br2)$(li)Their own inventory$(li)Nearby dropped items$(li)Containers near their home$(li)Ripe crops they can harvest$(br2)Each source can be toggled in the config.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.hunger.page5: The reliable setup is a stocked chest near the villager's home plus a food source that refills it. For farmers this is automatic once the farm is producing. For other jobs you'll want a farmer nearby, or hand out food yourself.$(br2)Better food restores more hunger. Golden carrots go a long way; raw potatoes do not.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.thirst.page1: Thirst runs from 0 to 20. Villagers start at 20 and lose points over time. Below 3, they slow down. At 0, they take damage every 2 seconds.$(br2)Thirst only appears if a compatible thirst mod is installed (Thirst Was Taken or Legendary Survival Overhaul).
townstead.guide.entry.needs.thirst.page2: Five bands from safe to critical:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Quenched$() (16-20) - small mood boost$(li)$(l)Hydrated$() (9-15) - no effect$(li)$(l)Thirsty$() (5-8) - mood drops, seeks a drink$(li)$(l)Parched$() (1-4) - speed -20%%$(li)$(l)Dehydrated$() (0) - takes damage, large mood penalty
townstead.guide.entry.needs.thirst.page3: Villagers drink from any item their thirst mod recognizes. The specifics depend on which mod is installed. See the Integrations section for details.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.thirst.page4: Stock water containers near the villager's home. The type of container depends on your thirst mod.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.energy.page1: Energy is displayed as a bar from 100 down to 0. Internally it tracks fatigue from 0 to 20. Fatigue rises from activity and falls from sleep.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.energy.page2: Energy has five bands:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Energized$() (0-3 fatigue) - mood boost$(li)$(l)Alert$() (4-7) - no effect$(li)$(l)Tired$() (8-11) - speed -10%%$(li)$(l)Drowsy$() (12-15) - speed -20%%$(li)$(l)Exhausted$() (16+) - speed -30%%, collapse at 20
townstead.guide.entry.needs.energy.page3: Activity adds fatigue at different rates:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Work$() - fastest$(li)$(l)Socializing$() - moderate$(li)$(l)Idle$() - slowest
Combat doubles the fatigue rate. A well-aligned shift schedule reduces accumulation by 25%%.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.energy.page4: Sleep is the main way to recover:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Bed, shift-aligned$() - recovers 1.25 per interval$(li)$(l)Bed, misaligned$() - recovers 0.6$(li)$(l)No bed$() - recovers 0.05 (barely anything)$(li)$(l)Collapsed$() - recovers 0.4 (automatic, can't move)$(br2)A collapsed villager stops until fatigue drops below 18.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.energy.page5: Each villager has a chronotype: $(l)Early Bird$(), $(l)Standard$(), or $(l)Night Owl$(). When their shift schedule matches their chronotype, they're "aligned":$(br2)$(li)Aligned: fatigue builds 25%% slower$(li)Misaligned: fatigue builds 25%% faster$(li)Aligned bed rest recovers roughly twice as fast
On the shift screen, a soft band marks each villager's preferred 8-hour sleep window. Schedule $(l)Rest$() over that band, and $(l)Work$() outside it, to keep them aligned.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.energy.page6: $(l)Early Bird$() sleeps roughly 6 PM to 2 AM.$(br)$(l)Standard$() sleeps roughly 8 PM to 4 AM.$(br)$(l)Night Owl$() sleeps roughly 11 PM to 7 AM.$(br2)Chronotype is derived from a villager's MCA personality. Check the band on their row in the shift screen to see which one they are.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.energy.page7: Items tagged as energy-restoring (like coffee) restore 5 fatigue instantly. Useful for getting a drowsy villager through their shift.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.caring_for_y: When a young villager's hunger drops below 12, nearby adults bring them food. Adults search up to 48 blocks for food sources (ground items and containers by default; crop harvesting is an opt-in fallback) and walk it over.
Same system for thirst. If a thirst mod is installed and a young villager's thirst drops below 12, adults bring drinks.$(br2)Only active when a compatible thirst mod is present.
Parents always help their own children. Non-parent villagers help too, but only if no parent is within 48 blocks and the helper isn't crabby or exhausted.
townstead.guide.entry.needs.reading_stat: Open the dialogue screen with a villager to see their needs. Three icons appear below the villager's name.

Hover over any icon for a tooltip with the exact value and state name:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Hunger$() - always visible, shows 0-100 and state$(li)$(l)Thirst$() - only if a thirst mod is installed, shows 0-20$(li)$(l)Energy$() - always visible, shows percentage (100 to 0)$(li)$(l)Community Spirit$() - shown on the village Spirit page
The icons change color to match the state:$(br2)$(li)Green/blue - healthy$(li)White - neutral$(li)Gold - warning$(li)Red - critical
townstead.guide.entry.professions.overvi: Townstead expands several MCA professions:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Farmer$() - works a Field Post farm$(li)$(l)Butcher$() - smokes meat, with extra Butchery work when that mod is installed$(li)$(l)Fisherman$() - catches fish from nearby water$(li)$(l)Shepherd$() - shears sheep in livestock pens$(li)$(l)Leatherworker$() - works with hides and leather supplies

A few jobs appear only when their supporting mod is installed:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Cook$() - Farmer's Delight kitchens$(li)$(l)Barista$() - Rustic Delight cafes$(br2)Every job still follows the villager's shift schedule and gains XP from completed work.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.assign: Open the MCA Blueprint Screen and click "Professions" on the right side. This shows the profession assignment UI where you can assign or change a villager's job.

Each profession needs the right workstation placed in the village:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Farmer$() - composter$(li)$(l)Butcher$() - smoker$(li)$(l)Cook$() - Kitchen building (requires Farmer's Delight)$(li)$(l)Barista$() - Cafe building (requires Rustic Delight)$(br2)The village scanner checks within 128 blocks for available workstation slots.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.leveli: Every profession has 5 tiers, from Novice to Master. Higher tiers unlock better efficiency.
Villagers earn XP by completing work tasks. Each profession earns at its own rate. There's a daily XP cap to prevent power-leveling and keep progression gradual.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.shifts: Each villager has a 24-hour schedule divided into four activities:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Work$() - performs their job$(li)$(l)Idle$() - wanders, no tasks$(li)$(l)Meet$() - socializes with other villagers$(li)$(l)Rest$() - sleeps or heads home
By default, villagers follow the vanilla schedule: work during the day, socialize around midday, rest in the evening.

Open the MCA Blueprint Screen and click "Shifts". A full-screen scheduler appears with a row per villager and a 24-hour grid.$(br2)Click any cell to cycle the activity, or pick one from the legend and click to paint. "Reset All" restores the default.

Each row has a template button on the right showing the current schedule's name, or $(l)Custom: name$() if it's been hand-edited.$(br2)Click the button to open the template manager: $(l)Load$() applies a template, $(l)Duplicate$() copies it, $(l)Save As Template$() saves the current row, $(l)Delete$() removes user templates.

To apply a template to several villagers at once, tick the checkbox on each row, then use the $(l)Apply template to N selected…$() button in the header. The "All" button toggles every row on the current page.
Villagers who sleep during their preferred-sleep hours recover energy faster. The soft band over the grid shows each villager's chronotype window. See the Energy entry for details.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.farmer: Farmers work Field Post farms. Their workstation is the composter. Any farmer whose composter sits inside a Field Post's range will follow that post's plan.
Farmers till, plant, water, harvest, and groom based on the Field Post plan. See the Farming section for full details.

Each villager's personality affects how they farm. Some push the plan harder, expanding tilled coverage faster; others take longer breaks between tasks. You can see a villager's personality in the dialogue screen.$(br2)Greedy farmers expand fastest and carry fewer seeds in reserve. Careful personalities like Gloomy, Sensitive, and Anxious expand slower but hold extra seeds in reserve so they don't stall when supplies run low.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.butche: Butchers smoke meat at a smoker. They fetch raw meat, fuel the smoker, and collect the cooked output.
A smoker block within 12 blocks, raw meat in a nearby chest, and fuel (coal, charcoal, etc.) in a nearby chest.
The butcher loads raw meat and fuel into the smoker, waits for it to cook, then collects the result and stocks it into nearby storage.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.fisher: Fishermen work from barrels, their workstation, and nearby fishable water. If there's a dock in your village, they'll prefer to cast from there. Otherwise, they'll use large bodies of water near the barrel.

Fishermen need a fishing rod to work. Any fishing rod will work, even Starcatcher's if you have it installed. When their inventory is filled, they will head back and drop it off in a nearby barrel or other storage container.
If a fisherman stops working, check the status tooltip or nearby chat. Common causes are no rod, no reachable water, no valid fishing spot, or a full inventory.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.shephe: Shepherds primarily take care of shearing sheep. If you give them shears and build a Livestock Pen, they will shear the sheep inside when there's enough wool to do so.
Livestock Pens are where shepherds look for sheep. After shearing, the shepherd will drop the wool off in Wool Sheds. If there is no Wool Shed, they'll use nearby storage containers.
Shears used by shepherds can degrade like when you use them. Compatible shears from other mods can also be used by the shepherd.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.leathe: Leatherworkers produce leather by tanning skins. In vanilla, they will not do much, but with the Butchery compat and a tannery building, they'll automate the tanning process.
Nearby storage matters. If the leatherworker can reach the right inputs and a place to put finished stock, they'll keep cycling through available jobs during work hours.
When Butchery is installed, leatherworkers can use the Tannery path and skin racks. Hides from butcher work can be processed into leather goods.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.cook.p: Cooks prepare meals at Farmer's Delight cooking stations: stoves, cooking pots, skillets, cutting boards, and campfires. This profession is only available when Farmer's Delight is installed.
Cooks work in kitchen buildings (L1 through L5):$(br2)$(li)$(l)L1-L2$() - 1 cook slot$(li)$(l)L3-L4$() - 2 cook slots$(li)$(l)L5$() - 3 cook slots

Cooks pick a recipe from the Farmer's Delight catalog, gather ingredients from nearby storage, and work the appropriate station. Each recipe routes to whichever block it was authored for, so a cook will move between stove, pot, skillet, cutting board, and campfire as the menu demands.
townstead.guide.entry.professions.barist: Baristas brew drinks using cooking pots. This profession is only available when Rustic Delight is installed.
Baristas work in cafe buildings (L1 through L5):$(br2)$(li)$(l)L1-L2$() - 1 barista slot$(li)$(l)L3-L4$() - 2 barista slots$(li)$(l)L5$() - 3 barista slots
Baristas brew drinks at cooking pots, selecting from available recipes and gathering ingredients from nearby storage.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.overv: Townstead detects installed mods automatically. No configuration needed.$(br2)$(li)$(l)Farmer's Delight$()$(li)$(l)Farming for Blockheads$()$(li)$(l)Legendary Survival Overhaul$()$(li)$(l)Thirst Was Taken$()$(li)$(l)Rustic Delight$()$(li)$(l)Butchery$()$(li)$(l)Starcatcher$()$(li)$(l)Traveler's Titles$()

When Townstead loads, it checks which mods are present and enables matching features. Mod-specific buildings, trades, tools, and title hooks are only registered when the required mod is installed.$(br2)Click on any mod's entry in this category for details on what it adds.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.butch: Butchery adds more work for butchers and leatherworkers. Butchers can process carcasses, smoke meat, and use Butchery items in trades. Leatherworkers can use the tannery and skin racks to process hides.

$(l)Butcher Shop$() tier 1: smoker, butcher's table, and storage.$(br2)$(l)Butcher Shop$() tier 2: adds a basin, hook, and counter.$(br2)$(l)Butcher Shop$() tier 3: adds the grinder, skin rack, pestle, and freezer.

$(l)Slaughter Pen$(): fenced area for livestock selected for slaughter.$(br2)$(l)Slaughterhouse$(): hooks, basins, grates, and storage for carcass work.$(br2)$(l)Smokehouse$(): extra smoking space.$(br2)$(l)Tannery$(): hide processing.

Slaughter Pens mark livestock selected for slaughter. A hook in a carcass-capable building, such as a tier 2+ Butcher Shop or a Slaughterhouse, is still required so the carcass has somewhere to go. Baby animals, named animals, and pets are never targeted.

$(l)Apprentice$(): specialty raw cuts.$(br2)$(l)Journeyman$(): sausages, minced meats, hides.$(br2)$(l)Expert$(): cooked specialty cuts and organs.$(br2)$(l)Master$(): cooked ham, blood sausage, leg of lamb.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.starc: Fishermen can equip Starcatcher rods and reel in Starcatcher fish from their registered catch pool, respecting biome, elevation, fluid, and bait restrictions just like a player. They skip the minigame and don't earn golden, perfect, treasure, or tournament rewards. If nothing in the Starcatcher pool is legal at the cast location, the villager falls back to normal vanilla fishing loot.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.trave: When Traveler's Titles is installed, entering a village can show a title based on that village's Community Spirit. The title can also show the village population.
This integration does not add blocks or jobs. Townstead provides the village title, and Traveler's Titles displays it.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.farme: Farmer's Delight integration is the largest. It unlocks:$(br2)$(li)Rich Soil as a soil type in the Plot Planner$(li)The Cook profession (stove workstation)$(li)Knife-based grooming (tall grass drops rope)$(li)Stove damage fix (villagers don't burn on stoves)
"Rich Soil" appears as a soil option in the Plot Planner. Farmers till dirt into farmland, then upgrade it to rich soil automatically.
FD crops like rice and tomatoes are fully supported. Rice uses water-based planting. Tomatoes and mushroom colonies support partial harvesting.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.farmi: Farming for Blockheads adds three fertilizer soil types to the Plot Planner:$(br2)$(li)$(l)Green Fertilizer$() - bonus crop yield$(li)$(l)Red Fertilizer$() - faster growth$(li)$(l)Yellow Fertilizer$() - trample protection
When Farming for Blockheads is installed, these options appear alongside the standard soil types. Farmers apply the selected fertilizer to farmland cells as part of their tilling routine.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.legen: LSO provides the thirst system that Townstead's villager thirst hooks into. If both LSO and Thirst Was Taken are installed, LSO takes priority.
Townstead reads LSO's hydration values for drinks. Hydration quality maps to LSO's purity system: purified water gives the best restoration, rain water is moderate, normal water is basic.
Villagers can drink from LSO canteens. Townstead adds campfire purification recipes so villagers can purify water.
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.thirs: Thirst Was Taken provides a thirst system that Townstead's villager thirst hooks into. Only active if LSO is not installed.
Townstead reads TWT's hydration and purity values. Water bottles, canteens, and other TWT drinks all work.
Water purity matters. Low-purity water can cause nausea or poison effects on villagers, scaled by TWT's purity levels (0-3).
townstead.guide.entry.integrations.rusti: Rustic Delight extends Farmer's Delight with cafe buildings and the Barista profession:$(br2)$(li)Cafe buildings L1 through L5$(li)The Barista profession (requires both FD and RD)$(li)7 additional drink recipes for baristas
Baristas can brew coffee variants (dark, milk, chocolate, honey, syrup), syrup, and standard coffee.
If Thirst Was Taken is also installed, RD drinks provide hydration values. Skipped if LSO is present instead.
townstead.guide.entry.village.community_: Community Spirit is a village-wide readout based on recognized buildings. Each building can add points to one or more spirit types, such as Nautical, Pastoral, Industrious, or Scholar.
Open the MCA Blueprint Screen, go to Status, then open the Spirit page. The list view shows each spirit's points and share. The radar view shows the same information as a chart.

A strong single spirit earns a tier name such as Dockside, Farmstead, or Foundry. Two strong spirits can blend into names like Saltmarsh or Artisan Market. Broad, balanced villages use mixed names instead.
traitDescription.townstead_roots:immorta: Ageless, and cannot be killed by any worldly harm.
townstead.heritage.title: Heritage
townstead.heritage.tooltip.carrier_wild: Shows %s, but only one copy — some children may not inherit it.
townstead.heritage.tooltip.carrier: Shows %s, but carries a hidden %s that can pass to children.
